sections.plain_summary: Sucrase-isomaltase deficiency (SID) is a genetic condition in which the small intestine lacks enough of the enzyme sucrase-isomaltase to break down sucrose (table sugar) and the starch digestion products maltose and isomaltose. Undigested sugars pass into the colon, where bacteria ferment them, producing gas, bloating, cramping, and diarrhea. It is frequently mistaken for IBS, functional diarrhea, or carbohydrate intolerances of other kinds, and many people with SID go years without a correct diagnosis.
sections.detailed_summary:
Sucrase-isomaltase (SI) is a brush-border enzyme embedded in the lining of the small intestine. It performs two distinct jobs: sucrase cleaves sucrose into glucose and fructose, and isomaltase cleaves the alpha-1,6 branch points of starch digestion products (isomaltose, limit dextrins). When SI activity is absent or severely reduced — due to genetic variants in the SI gene on chromosome 3q25-26 — neither sucrose nor the isomaltose fraction of starch is absorbed efficiently. The osmotic load and bacterial fermentation that follow cause watery or loose stools, gas, bloating, and abdominal pain that are tightly linked to dietary carbohydrate intake.

The condition exists on a spectrum. Congenital sucrase-isomaltase deficiency (CSID) is the classical form, present from birth, and tends to be most severe. It is caused by biallelic loss-of-function variants in the SI gene. However, research in the past decade — particularly studies by Treem, Husein, and the team behind the Sucraid trials — has established that a significant proportion of adults diagnosed with IBS carry heterozygous hypomorphic variants in the SI gene that reduce but do not abolish enzyme activity. This partial or 'compound heterozygous' form may be far more common than the congenital form and is likely underdiagnosed in the adult IBS population.

Diagnosis historically required a small intestinal biopsy with direct enzyme assay or a disaccharidase panel from endoscopic biopsy, which remains the gold standard. A 13C-sucrose breath test is emerging as a less invasive option. Genetic testing for SI variants is increasingly available but requires careful interpretation because not all variants are fully characterized. An empirical sucrose elimination trial is often used as a first step in self-directed investigation.

Management centers on reducing dietary sucrose and, to a lesser degree, starch-derived isomaltose, and on enzyme replacement with sacrosidase (Sucraid), a yeast-derived sucrase enzyme approved in the United States and Canada. Some individuals with partial deficiency manage well with diet modification alone. Starch tolerance varies substantially depending on the degree of isomaltase loss.

prevalence.summary: Relatively rare in its severe congenital form but potentially much more common in milder genetic variants that present as adult IBS.
prevalence.estimate: Classical CSID: approximately 1 in 5,000 in North American and European populations; up to 1 in 10 in certain Indigenous Arctic populations (Greenlandic Inuit, Alaskan Native). Partial SID variants: estimated in up to 4–9% of people diagnosed with IBS in some North American studies.
prevalence.notes: Prevalence figures for the severe congenital form are reasonably well established. The prevalence of hypomorphic SI variants in the general population and among IBS patients is an area of active research and estimates vary. The figure of ~4% of IBS patients carrying functionally relevant SI variants comes from the 2020 study by Husein et al. and related work; it is plausible but not yet universally accepted. Underdiagnosis is likely significant given the reliance on biopsy-based enzyme assays that are not routinely ordered.

subgroups.name: Congenital sucrase-isomaltase deficiency (CSID)
subgroups.description: Classical form caused by homozygous or compound heterozygous loss-of-function mutations in the SI gene, resulting in near-total absence of SI enzyme activity. Onset from infancy when sucrose-containing foods are introduced.
distinguishing_features: Severe, watery diarrhea beginning in infancy when fruit juices or sucrose-containing formula are introduced
Failure to thrive in infancy in severe cases
Near-zero sucrase activity on disaccharidase biopsy panel
Full diagnosis typically made in pediatric gastroenterology

subgroups.name: Partial or adult-onset SID (hypomorphic variants)
subgroups.description: Caused by heterozygous or compound heterozygous SI variants that reduce but do not abolish enzyme activity. Symptoms may be milder, more variable, or emerge in adulthood. This group overlaps heavily with people diagnosed with IBS-D or IBS-M.
distinguishing_features: Symptoms often present in adolescence or adulthood, not necessarily infancy
Reduced — not absent — sucrase activity on biopsy panel
May be triggered or unmasked by dietary changes, gut infections, or stress
Often diagnosed as IBS before SID is considered
Symptoms may partially improve on a low-FODMAP diet that incidentally reduces sucrose, masking the true diagnosis

subgroups.name: Secondary sucrase-isomaltase deficiency
subgroups.description: Acquired, temporary reduction in SI activity due to damage to the small intestinal brush border from another cause, such as coeliac disease, Crohn's disease, post-infectious enteropathy, or severe SIBO. Resolves when the underlying injury heals.
distinguishing_features: Not genetic — no SI gene variants expected
Onset linked to a known gut insult or illness
Sucrase activity recovers with treatment of the primary condition
Important to distinguish from genetic SID before committing to lifelong management

symptoms.symptom: Chronic or recurrent watery or loose diarrhea
symptoms.notes: Classically triggered within 30–90 minutes of consuming sucrose or sucrose-containing foods. In infants, onset coincides with introduction of fruit or sucrose-containing foods.
Typically occurs in the lower abdomen; worsens after high-sucrose meals such as fruit juice, candy, sweetened drinks, or baked goods.
Results from bacterial fermentation of undigested sucrose and isomaltose in the colon, producing hydrogen and methane gas.
Often described as pronounced and foul-smelling; worsens after starchy or sugary meals.
May accompany acute episodes, particularly after large sugar loads.
Can be socially limiting; often follows meals containing sucrose within an hour or two.

symptoms.symptom: Failure to thrive or poor weight gain in infancy
symptoms.notes: Specific to the congenital form presenting in infancy; often the presenting feature that prompts investigation.
May relate to poor nutrient absorption during frequent diarrhea episodes and the cumulative burden of chronic symptoms.

symptoms.symptom: Nutritional deficiencies (iron, B vitamins, fat-soluble vitamins)
symptoms.notes: Secondary to chronic malabsorption and frequent loose stools; more common in severe or long-undiagnosed cases.

symptoms.symptom: Irritability and behavioral symptoms in children
symptoms.notes: Reported in pediatric CSID; may reflect chronic gut discomfort or nutritional deficits.

key_patterns: Symptoms that worsen specifically after consuming sucrose (table sugar, fruit juice, sweets, honey) and improve on a sugar-restricted diet
Symptoms that also worsen after heavily starchy meals (bread, pasta, rice, potatoes) — suggesting isomaltase component
Partial or incomplete response to a low-FODMAP diet, because sucrose is not a FODMAP but is not tolerated
Family history of similar digestive symptoms, particularly if of Greenlandic Inuit, Alaskan Native, or Northern European ancestry
Onset of symptoms in childhood or adolescence without a clear structural explanation
Low or absent sucrase band on a small intestinal disaccharidase assay from endoscopic biopsy

at_home.name: Sucrose elimination trial
at_home.description: Remove all sucrose and obvious sugar sources (table sugar, fruit juice, honey, high-sugar fruits, processed foods with added sugars, sucrose-containing medications) for 2–4 weeks. A food and symptom diary helps track changes precisely. If symptoms improve substantially, reintroducing a sucrose challenge (e.g., a glass of fruit juice or a sugary drink on an empty stomach) and noting a return of symptoms within 1–2 hours provides supporting evidence.
at_home.safety_notes: Elimination of sucrose is nutritionally safe for most people for a short trial period. Those on restricted diets for other reasons should ensure adequate caloric intake. This is an exploratory step, not a diagnostic confirmation — a positive result should prompt clinical testing.

at_home.name: Structured symptom and food diary
at_home.description: Record all foods and drinks consumed alongside symptom timing, severity, and type for at least 2 weeks before any elimination. Look specifically for a temporal relationship between sugary or starchy foods and symptoms occurring 30–120 minutes later. Note whether fizzy drinks, sweets, fruit, or baked goods are consistent triggers.
at_home.safety_notes: No risks. Detailed diaries significantly improve the quality of clinical consultations and help distinguish SID from other carbohydrate intolerances.

clinical.name: Small intestinal disaccharidase assay (biopsy-based)
clinical.description: Multiple biopsy specimens are taken from the duodenum or jejunum during upper endoscopy (gastroscopy) and sent to a specialized laboratory for direct measurement of enzyme activities: sucrase, isomaltase, lactase, maltase, and palatinase. Low sucrase activity — typically below 25–30 U/g protein, though laboratory reference ranges vary — confirms the diagnosis. This is the most definitive test available.
clinical.availability: Requires upper endoscopy, which is ordered by a gastroenterologist. The biopsy specimens must be sent to a laboratory that performs the full disaccharidase panel — not all hospital pathology labs offer this. In the United States, specialized labs such as Baylor St. Luke's GI Motility lab or similar academic centers process these samples. Ordering clinicians may need prompting to specify the full disaccharidase panel.

clinical.name: 13C-sucrose breath test
clinical.description: An orally ingested dose of 13C-labeled sucrose is given, and breath samples are collected at intervals to measure 13CO2 excretion, reflecting sucrose hydrolysis and absorption. Reduced 13CO2 production indicates impaired sucrase activity. Less invasive than biopsy and potentially more accessible.
clinical.availability: Not yet widely available outside specialist centers and research settings in many countries. Available at some academic gastroenterology units in the United States and Europe. May become a standard screening tool as access expands.

clinical.name: Hydrogen/methane breath test with sucrose substrate
clinical.description: Similar in principle to lactose or fructose breath tests. A sucrose substrate is ingested and breath hydrogen/methane measured at intervals. Elevated hydrogen or methane suggests colonic fermentation of undigested sucrose. Less specific than the disaccharidase assay — results can be confounded by SIBO or rapid transit — but more accessible.
clinical.availability: Available at most gastroenterology units and some functional medicine or private clinics that offer breath testing. Some direct-to-consumer breath test providers offer sucrose breath tests with a home collection kit, though clinical interpretation is recommended.

clinical.name: SI gene genetic testing / variant panel
clinical.description: Sequencing of the SI gene to identify known pathogenic or likely pathogenic variants. Panels vary in the number of variants assessed. Useful as a supporting tool and increasingly used to identify the partial/hypomorphic variants associated with adult IBS-like presentations.
clinical.availability: Available through clinical genetics services and increasingly through specialist GI genetic panels. Some direct-to-consumer genetic testing companies include SI variants on carbohydrate intolerance panels, though variant interpretation requires clinical expertise. Not yet a standalone diagnostic standard.

clinical.name: Empirical sacrosidase (Sucraid) trial
clinical.description: In some clinical settings, particularly in pediatrics, a therapeutic trial with the enzyme replacement sacrosidase (Sucraid) is used diagnostically. A clear and reproducible symptom response — improvement when taking the enzyme with sucrose-containing meals and return of symptoms when the enzyme is stopped — provides strong supporting evidence for SID.
clinical.availability: Sucraid is a prescription medication in the United States and Canada. Its high cost and restricted availability mean this approach is more common in pediatric than adult practice.

related_conditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)
related_conditions.explanation: SID, particularly the partial adult form, is frequently misdiagnosed as IBS-D or IBS-M. The symptom profiles overlap substantially, and sucrose is not assessed by standard IBS workups. Studies suggest several percent of IBS patients carry functionally relevant SI variants.

related_conditions.name: Lactase deficiency
related_conditions.explanation: Both are brush-border enzyme deficiencies of the small intestine. They can coexist, particularly in secondary deficiency states where intestinal damage reduces multiple enzyme activities simultaneously. Distinguishing them requires careful dietary testing or a full disaccharidase panel.

related_conditions.name: Fructose malabsorption
related_conditions.explanation: Sucrose is composed of glucose and fructose. When sucrase activity is impaired, the undigested sucrose load in the colon mimics fructose malabsorption symptomatically and can produce a positive fructose breath test, leading to misdiagnosis. However, fructose malabsorption relates to GLUT-5 transporter capacity, not enzyme activity.

related_conditions.name: Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO)
related_conditions.explanation: Chronic delivery of undigested sugars to the upper GI tract can alter the microbial environment and potentially promote bacterial overgrowth. SIBO can also secondarily reduce SI enzyme activity by damaging the brush border. The two conditions can coexist and complicate each other's diagnosis.

related_conditions.name: Coeliac disease
related_conditions.explanation: Active coeliac disease damages the small intestinal villi and brush border, causing secondary (acquired) reductions in sucrase-isomaltase and other disaccharidase activities. This secondary SID resolves with a gluten-free diet and intestinal healing. Coeliac disease should be excluded before attributing low disaccharidase activity to a genetic cause.

related_conditions.name: Crohn's disease
related_conditions.explanation: Small bowel Crohn's disease can damage the enterocytes responsible for producing sucrase-isomaltase, causing secondary enzyme deficiency. Persistent GI symptoms in Crohn's patients in apparent remission may reflect SID rather than active inflammation.

related_conditions.name: Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency (EPI)
related_conditions.explanation: Both EPI and SID cause malabsorption with diarrhea, bloating, and gas after eating. EPI primarily affects fat digestion, while SID affects sugar digestion, but the symptom overlap is sufficient to cause diagnostic confusion, particularly when fat intake coincides with sugar intake.

related_conditions.name: Congenital glucose-galactose malabsorption
related_conditions.explanation: Another rare congenital carbohydrate absorption disorder presenting in infancy. Distinguished from CSID by the specific sugars that trigger symptoms and by different underlying mechanisms (SGLT1 transporter defect versus enzyme deficiency).

related_conditions.name: Aldolase B deficiency (hereditary fructose intolerance)
related_conditions.explanation: Both conditions cause adverse reactions to sucrose (which contains fructose). Hereditary fructose intolerance is a liver enzyme disorder with more systemic and potentially severe consequences, while SID is purely intestinal. The distinction has important management implications.

related_conditions.name: Gut dysbiosis
related_conditions.explanation: Chronic colonic fermentation of undigested sucrose and isomaltose may alter the composition of the gut microbiome over time, potentially promoting dysbiosis. This is a plausible secondary consequence rather than a cause.

strategies.name: Sucrose restriction diet
strategies.description: Reducing or eliminating dietary sucrose is the cornerstone of SID management. This involves avoiding table sugar, fruit juice, honey, high-sugar fruits, most commercially processed foods with added sugar, and sucrose-containing syrups. A registered dietitian familiar with SID or rare carbohydrate disorders can help design a nutritionally complete diet. Starch restriction (particularly foods high in isomaltose-yielding starches) may also be needed depending on isomaltase activity.
strategies.notes: Tolerance levels vary between individuals and between the congenital and partial forms. Some people with partial SID tolerate small amounts of sucrose. A dietitian's guidance is important to avoid unnecessary over-restriction, which can compromise nutrition and quality of life.

strategies.name: Sacrosidase (Sucraid) enzyme replacement
strategies.description: Sacrosidase is a yeast-derived sucrase enzyme taken orally with each sucrose-containing meal or snack. It supplements the deficient enzyme at the brush border. It is FDA-approved for CSID and has strong evidence in the congenital form. Some clinicians use it off-label in adults with partial SID.
strategies.notes: Sacrosidase is very expensive in the United States and access can be a barrier without strong insurance coverage. It does not replace isomaltase activity, so starch tolerance may still be limited. It is formulated in a sucrose-free, preservative-free solution and is sensitive to heat. Patients with yeast hypersensitivity should use with caution.

strategies.name: Low-starch dietary modification
strategies.description: For individuals with significant isomaltase deficiency alongside sucrase deficiency, reducing intake of highly processed starches — particularly those with a high isomaltose yield such as waxy corn starch and some potato products — may reduce symptoms. Whole intact grains may be better tolerated than refined starch products in some individuals.
strategies.notes: The isomaltase component of SID is less well characterized in terms of dietary management guidance. Individual tolerance testing under dietitian supervision is recommended rather than blanket starch restriction.

strategies.name: Digestive enzyme supplements (non-prescription)
strategies.description: Some over-the-counter digestive enzyme blends contain sucrase or invertase activity derived from fungal or plant sources. These may provide modest benefit for people with partial SID who cannot access or afford Sucraid. Quality and potency vary widely between products.
strategies.notes: No randomized controlled trial data exist for OTC sucrase supplements specifically in SID. Some individuals report benefit; others do not. These should not be used as a substitute for diagnosis or in place of prescription sacrosidase in confirmed CSID. Discuss with a clinician before relying on these.

strategies.name: Nutritional supplementation
strategies.description: People with long-undiagnosed SID may have developed nutritional deficiencies from years of malabsorption and dietary restriction. Iron, B12, folate, vitamin D, and other fat-soluble vitamins may need supplementation guided by blood testing.
strategies.notes: Supplementation should be guided by laboratory testing rather than assumed. A dietitian or physician with an interest in malabsorption conditions is helpful here.

strategies.name: Structured food and symptom tracking
strategies.description: Maintaining a detailed diary linking food intake to symptom timing and severity allows individuals to identify their personal threshold and specific trigger foods. This is particularly useful in partial SID where the dose-response relationship with sucrose is more nuanced.
strategies.notes: Useful both as a management tool and as supporting data for clinical consultations.

things_to_avoid: Fruit juices and sweetened soft drinks — very high in sucrose or free fructose, among the most reliable triggers
Table sugar, icing sugar, caster sugar, and products listing sucrose as a main ingredient
Honey and agave syrup — high in fructose and sucrose
High-sugar processed foods: confectionery, cakes, biscuits, flavored yogurts, cereals with added sugar
Sucrose-containing liquid medications and syrups — a frequently overlooked source
Relying on a low-FODMAP diet as the primary management approach — it does not restrict sucrose and will miss this condition
Assuming all starchy foods are equally problematic without individual testing — unnecessary restriction can compromise nutrition
Delaying enzyme replacement (Sucraid) in confirmed congenital CSID, particularly in children, where growth and development may be affected

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Most gastroenterologists do not routinely test for SID, and many are unfamiliar with the adult partial form. People can spend years on IBS management that will never fully work because the underlying enzyme deficiency is never identified.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: It is easier to accept an IBS label and try symptom management than to pursue obscure enzyme testing that requires specialist referral and endoscopy. Many people hope that diet tweaks or probiotics will eventually work.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Individuals who request a full disaccharidase panel via endoscopic biopsy — and who find a gastroenterologist willing to order it — often get a clear result within weeks of the procedure. A confirmed diagnosis completely redirects management toward something that actually targets the cause.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: A low-FODMAP diet can partially mask SID by incidentally reducing some sugary foods, creating the impression that IBS is being managed — while the true diagnosis remains invisible.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Partial improvement feels like progress and discourages further investigation. The low-FODMAP framework does not include sucrose as a target, so neither patients nor their dietitians are prompted to look further.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Recognizing that sucrose is not a FODMAP — and specifically testing sucrose restriction as a separate dietary variable — reveals whether SID is the missing piece. People who do this often describe a degree of symptom control they never achieved on low-FODMAP alone.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Sacrosidase (Sucraid) is extremely expensive in the United States, and cost can be a genuine barrier to optimal management even after a confirmed diagnosis. There is currently no generic alternative.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: People hope cost issues will resolve, or they delay pursuing diagnosis if treatment seems unaffordable.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Dietary sucrose restriction — while demanding — can achieve meaningful symptom control for many people with partial SID without enzyme replacement. For confirmed CSID, patient assistance programs through the manufacturer and aggressive insurance prior authorization processes have helped some patients access the medication at reduced cost.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: The genetic picture of SID is complex and not fully characterized. A 'negative' genetic test does not rule out functionally significant SID — only the disaccharidase enzyme assay from biopsy can confirm reduced enzyme activity.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: People often expect genetic testing to give a definitive answer. A negative or 'variant of uncertain significance' result can falsely reassure both patient and clinician.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Individuals who understand that the biopsy-based enzyme assay remains the diagnostic standard pursue that test directly rather than waiting for genetic results to resolve. This avoids years of diagnostic delay.

key_levers.lever: Obtain a confirmed diagnosis via disaccharidase biopsy panel
key_levers.why_it_matters: Without a confirmed diagnosis, management remains guesswork. The biopsy panel gives a quantitative measure of enzyme activity that definitively guides treatment decisions and communicates the severity of deficiency.
key_levers.how_to_start: Request a referral to a gastroenterologist specifically for upper endoscopy with a disaccharidase panel. Bring documentation of dietary patterns and symptom correlations to the consultation. If the first gastroenterologist is unfamiliar with the test, seek a second opinion at an academic medical center.
key_levers.timeline: Endoscopy can typically be arranged within weeks to a few months. Results from a specialized laboratory take 1–2 weeks after the procedure.

key_levers.lever: Implement a structured sucrose-restricted diet guided by a knowledgeable dietitian
key_levers.why_it_matters: Dietary sucrose restriction directly removes the substrate that causes symptoms. Done carefully with professional guidance, it is nutritionally sustainable and can produce dramatic symptom improvement without medication.
key_levers.how_to_start: Find a registered dietitian with experience in rare carbohydrate disorders, enzyme deficiencies, or pediatric GI nutrition (for CSID). The CSID Cares Foundation and similar organizations can provide dietitian referral resources. Begin with a clear elimination phase, then structured reintroduction to find personal tolerance thresholds.
key_levers.timeline: Clear symptom improvement is often seen within 1–2 weeks of sucrose elimination. Fine-tuning for starch tolerance and individual thresholds takes several months.

key_levers.lever: Enzyme replacement with sacrosidase (Sucraid) for confirmed CSID or partial SID with significant functional impairment
key_levers.why_it_matters: Replacing the missing enzyme at the point of digestion addresses the root cause directly, allowing a broader diet and reducing the social and nutritional burden of strict sucrose elimination.
key_levers.how_to_start: Discuss prescription sacrosidase with the treating gastroenterologist or pediatric GI specialist following confirmed diagnosis. Explore insurance coverage, manufacturer assistance programs, and prior authorization pathways proactively.
key_levers.timeline: Symptom response to sacrosidase is typically seen within days of correct dosing with sucrose-containing meals.

key_levers.lever: Address nutritional deficiencies accumulated during years of malabsorption
key_levers.why_it_matters: Correcting iron, B12, vitamin D, and other deficits improves energy, cognitive function, and overall wellbeing — areas that do not resolve from dietary change alone if stores are depleted.
key_levers.how_to_start: Request a comprehensive nutritional blood panel from a GP or internist, including ferritin, B12, folate, vitamin D, zinc, and a full blood count. Use results to guide targeted supplementation.
key_levers.timeline: Nutritional repletion typically takes 3–6 months for meaningful improvement in markers and symptoms.

common_mistakes: Treating SID like generic IBS with fiber supplements, probiotics, or antispasmodics — these do not address the enzyme deficiency and provide minimal lasting benefit
Restricting fructose (via low-FODMAP) while leaving sucrose unrestricted — the wrong carbohydrate is being targeted
Relying on genetic testing as a diagnostic endpoint and abandoning investigation when variants are absent or uncertain
Restricting all starches broadly and permanently without testing individual starch tolerance — this creates unnecessary nutritional restriction and reduced quality of life
Using Sucraid as an excuse to eat unlimited sucrose — enzyme replacement has dosing limits and does not provide unlimited sucrose hydrolysis capacity
Diagnosing secondary SID (caused by coeliac disease or Crohn's) as genetic SID without excluding the primary condition first
Failing to check sucrose content in medications, liquid supplements, and children's vitamins — a hidden sucrose source that can undermine dietary management

specialists.role: Primary specialist for ordering and interpreting the disaccharidase biopsy panel, performing upper endoscopy, and coordinating enzyme replacement prescription.
specialists.notes: Community gastroenterologists may not be familiar with SID in adults. Seeking care at an academic medical center or a center with a known interest in malabsorption disorders significantly increases the likelihood of appropriate testing being ordered. Pediatric gastroenterologists are generally more familiar with CSID.

specialists.type: Registered dietitian (specializing in GI or rare metabolic disorders)
specialists.role: Designs and monitors a nutritionally adequate sucrose-restricted diet, identifies hidden sucrose sources, manages starch tolerance testing, and addresses any nutritional deficiencies.
specialists.notes: Dietitians with experience in CSID or rare carbohydrate enzyme disorders are uncommon but exist, particularly in pediatric GI programs. The CSID Cares Foundation maintains a referral network. Standard IBS dietitians may not have familiarity with SID-specific dietary management.

specialists.role: The specialist most likely to be familiar with the congenital form and its management in children; also the most experienced prescribers of sacrosidase (Sucraid).
specialists.notes: Adults with long-undiagnosed CSID or partial SID may find that pediatric GI specialists (who may see adult patients or transition patients to adult care) have the most relevant clinical experience.

specialists.type: Clinical geneticist or metabolic disease specialist
specialists.role: Can provide interpretation of SI gene variants, family counseling, and guidance in complex genetic presentations.
specialists.notes: Most relevant when there is a family history or when genetic testing returns variants of uncertain significance that require further interpretation.

specialists.type: Functional medicine doctor or integrative gastroenterologist
specialists.role: May be more willing to pursue non-standard diagnostic pathways, including empirical dietary trials and ordering of specialist tests that mainstream clinicians might not consider.
specialists.notes: Variable quality and evidence-basis. Most useful as a complement to, not a replacement for, conventional GI investigation. Awareness of SID varies. Useful if mainstream gastroenterology has been dismissive.

patient_communities.name: CSID Cares Foundation
patient_communities.notes: The primary patient advocacy and information organization for CSID internationally. Provides dietitian referrals, physician lists, dietary resources, a sucrose-free product database, and a patient community. The most important single resource for people navigating this diagnosis.

patient_communities.name: CSID / Sucrase Isomaltase Deficiency Support Group
patient_communities.notes: An active Facebook group for patients and families managing CSID and SID. Useful for practical dietary advice, product recommendations, and connecting with others who understand the condition. Search 'CSID' on Facebook to find current active groups.

patient_communities.name: r/CSID
patient_communities.notes: A small but growing subreddit for people with CSID and related enzyme deficiencies. Useful for anecdotal experience, dietary tips, and navigating diagnosis.

patient_communities.name: Rare Digestive Disorders — HealthUnlocked
patient_communities.notes: Community forum covering rare GI conditions including SID. May be particularly relevant for UK-based patients navigating NHS pathways.

learning_resources.title: CSID Cares Foundation — Patient and Clinician Resources
learning_resources.description: The most comprehensive patient-facing resource for SID. Covers diagnosis, diet, Sucraid access, recipes, dietitian referrals, and physician finder. Essential first stop for anyone newly exploring this diagnosis.

learning_resources.title: Sucrase-Isomaltase Deficiency — GeneReviews (NCBI)
learning_resources.author_or_source: National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI) / NIH
learning_resources.description: The authoritative clinical genetics review of CSID. Covers molecular genetics, clinical features, diagnosis criteria, management, and genetic counseling. Written for clinicians but accessible to informed patients. Regularly updated.

learning_resources.title: Congenital Sucrase-Isomaltase Deficiency: Identification and Treatment
learning_resources.author_or_source: Treem WR — Journal of Pediatric Gastroenterology and Nutrition
learning_resources.description: A foundational review by one of the leading researchers in CSID. Covers pathophysiology, clinical presentation, and management. Useful for understanding the scientific basis of the condition. Available via PubMed.

learning_resources.title: Prevalence of Sucrase-Isomaltase Gene Mutations in Patients Diagnosed with Irritable Bowel Syndrome
learning_resources.author_or_source: Husein DM et al. — Alimentary Pharmacology & Therapeutics (2020)
learning_resources.description: Key study establishing that a meaningful subset of IBS patients carry functionally relevant SI gene variants. Important reading for anyone with an IBS diagnosis who suspects SID. Available via PubMed.

learning_resources.title: Sucraid (sacrosidase) Prescribing Information and Patient Guide
learning_resources.description: Official information on the prescription enzyme replacement sacrosidase, including dosing, access support, and patient assistance programs. Relevant for confirmed CSID patients and their clinicians.

learning_resources.title: The Specific Carbohydrate Diet — Breaking the Vicious Cycle
learning_resources.description: While not specifically written for SID, the Specific Carbohydrate Diet (SCD) eliminates sucrose and most complex starches and is used by some SID patients as a framework. The science has evolved since publication, but the book remains a useful practical resource for very low-sucrose eating. Should be read critically and adapted with dietitian guidance.

sections.relevance_signal: Chronic diarrhea, bloating, and gas that worsen specifically after eating sugar, starchy foods, or fruit — particularly since childhood — and that have not resolved despite IBS treatment or low-FODMAP diets.
